blocks:
Ayahs

سَأَلَ سَآئِلٌۢ بِعَذَابٍ وَاقِعٍ (١)
An asker asked for a punishment sure to befall (1)
لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَافِعٌ (٢)
—which none can avert from the faithless — (2)
مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ذِى ٱلْمَعَارِجِ (٣)
from Allah, Lord of the lofty stations. (3)
تَعْرُجُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِى يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُۥ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ (٤)
The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose span is fifty thousand years. (4)
فَٱصْبِرْ صَبْرًا جَمِيلًا (٥)
So be patient, with a patience that is graceful. (5)
إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُۥ بَعِيدًا (٦)
Indeed they see it to be far off, (6)
وَنَرَىٰهُ قَرِيبًا (٧)
and We see it to be near. (7)
يَوْمَ تَكُونُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ كَٱلْمُهْلِ (٨)
The day when the sky will be like molten copper, (8)
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ (٩)
and the mountains like [tufts of] dyed wool, (9)
وَلَا يَسْـَٔلُ حَمِيمٌ حَمِيمًا (١٠)
and no friend will inquire about [the welfare of his] friend, (10)
يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ ۚ يَوَدُّ ٱلْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِى مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِئِذٍۭ بِبَنِيهِ (١١)
[though] they will be placed within each other’s sight. The guilty one will wish he could ransom himself from the punishment of that day at the price of his children, (11)
وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَأَخِيهِ (١٢)
his spouse and his brother, (12)
وَفَصِيلَتِهِ ٱلَّتِى تُـْٔوِيهِ (١٣)
his kin, which had sheltered him (13)
وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يُنجِيهِ (١٤)
and all those who are upon the earth, if that might deliver him. (14)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهَا لَظَىٰ (١٥)
Never! Indeed, it is a blazing fire, (15)
نَزَّاعَةً لِّلشَّوَىٰ (١٦)
which strips away the scalp. (16)
تَدْعُوا۟ مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (١٧)
It invites him, who has turned back [from the truth] and forsaken [it], (17)
وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعَىٰٓ (١٨)
amassing [wealth] and hoarding [it]. (18)

(Commentary)

What the context (السياق) of the Surah provides is that it describes the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) in terms of the painful punishment prepared therein for the disbelievers (الكافرين). The Surah begins by mentioning the request of a petitioner who asked for a punishment from Allah for the disbelievers. It indicates that this punishment is a reality (واقع) that has no repeller (دافع), and that it is near, not far as they imagine. It then describes the day on which it occurs and the punishment prepared for them therein, and it excepts the believers (المؤمنين) who fulfilled the duties of true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

This context resembles the context of the Meccan Surahs, except that it is reported from some that His saying: وَالَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ "And those in whose wealth there is a known right" (70:24) is Medinan. Consideration supports this because its apparent meaning refers to the alms-tax (الزكاة), which was legislated in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة). The fact that this verse is Medinan entails that the verses surrounding it, which fall under the exception—namely fourteen verses from His saying: إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ "except the prayerful" (70:22) until His saying: فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ "they will be honored in gardens" (70:35)—are also Medinan, due to the unity in their context and the necessary connection of one part to the other.

The Medinan nature of these verses falling under the exception necessitates the Medinan nature of that from which they were excepted, which is at least three verses: His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً "Indeed man has been created covetous" (70:19) until His saying: مَنُوعاً "and when good visits him, [he is] grudging" (70:21).

Furthermore, His saying: فَما لِالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ "What is the matter with the faithless, that they gallop toward you" (70:36) is clearly branched from what precedes it, and what follows it until the end of the Surah possesses a single context; thus, these verses would also be Medinan.

From another perspective, the contents of this section of verses suit the state of the hypocrites (المنافقين) who were surrounding the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on the right and on the left in separate groups (عزين), and they were those who rejected some of what Allah revealed of the law (الحكم), especially His saying: أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ "Does every one of them greedily hope..." (70:38) etc., and His saying: عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ "to replace them with [others] better than them" (70:41) etc., as will come. The place where this hypocrisy (النفاق) manifested was Medina, not Mecca. There is no harm in referring to these people as "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا), for the like of this exists in Surah al-Tawbah and elsewhere.

Moreover, they have narrated that the Surah was revealed regarding the saying of the one who said: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنا حِجارَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ أَوِ ائْتِنا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ "O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down on us stones from the sky, or bring us a painful punishment" (8:32). It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse that its context and the context of what follows it is Medinan, not Meccan. However, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام that what is meant by the "known right" (الحق المعلوم) in the verse is a right that the owner of the wealth designates in his property other than the obligatory alms-tax (الزكاة المفروضة).

No weight is given to what is attributed to the consensus of the commentators (المفسرين) that the Surah is Meccan, as the disagreement is apparent; likewise, what is attributed to Ibn Abbas that it was revealed after Surah al-Haqqah.

His saying, the Exalted: سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِعٍ "A petitioner asked for a punishment bound to happen" (70:1). Asking (السؤال) here is in the sense of requesting (الطلب) and supplicating (الدعاء); therefore, it is made transitive with the particle bi (ب), as in His saying: يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِكُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ آمِنِينَ "They will call therein for every kind of fruit, secure" (44:55). It is also said that the verb implies the meaning of concern (الاهتمام) and care (الاعتناء), which is why it is made transitive with bi. It is also said that the bi is extra (زائدة) for emphasis (التأكيد). The outcome of these views is the same: it is a request for punishment from Allah out of disbelief (كفرا) and rebellion (عتوا).

It is also said that the bi is in the sense of 'an (عن - about), as in His saying: فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً "so ask someone who is aware about Him" (25:59). However, the claim that it means 'an in the cited verse is contested. Furthermore, the context of the subsequent verses, especially His saying: فَاصْبِرْ صَبْراً جَمِيلاً "So be patient with a graceful patience" (70:5), does not suit the "asking" being in the sense of inquiry (الاستفسار) or seeking information (الاستخبار).

Thus, the verse recounts the asking for punishment and the requesting of it by some of those who disbelieved out of tyranny (طغيانا) and faithlessness (كفرا). The requested punishment has been described with attributes that indicate the answering of the prayer by way of sarcasm (التهكم) and belittlement (التحقير), which is His saying: واقِعٍ "bound to happen" and His saying: لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ "which none can avert."

The meaning is: a petitioner from among the disbelievers asked for a punishment for the disbelievers from Allah that will surely strike them and fall upon them, and there is no averter for it; that is, it is happening to them whether he asked or did not ask. In this, there is a belittling response and an answering of his request sarcastically.

His saying, the Exalted: لِلْكافِرينَ لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ "for the faithless, which none can avert" (70:2). "For the faithless" (للكافرين) is connected to "punishment" (عذاب) and is an attribute for it, as is His saying: "which none can avert" (لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ). The meaning of the verse has already been indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنَ اللهِ ذِي الْمَعارِجِ "from Allah, Lord of the high degrees" (70:3). The prepositional phrase is connected to His saying: "averter" (دافع); that is, there is no averter for it from the side of Allah. It is well known that if it were to be averted, it would only be averted from the side of Allah, the Glorified. It is also possible that it is connected to His saying: "with a punishment" (بِعَذابٍ).

The "high degrees" (المعارج) is the plural of ma'raj (معرج), and they have interpreted it as places of ascent (المصاعد), which are the ranks (الدرجات). These are the stations of the celestial realm (مقامات الملكوت) to which the angels ascend upon their return to Allah, the Glorified, as interpreted by His subsequent saying: تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day..." (70:4). Thus, for Him, the Glorified, belong the ascents of the celestial realm and its stations, which are arranged in height and nobility, through which the angels and the Spirit ascend according to their proximity to Allah; these are not imaginary or conventional stations.

It is also said that what is meant by the "high degrees" (المعارج) are the ranks through which true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) ascend. The Exalted said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct" (35:10), and He said: وَلكِنْ يَنالُهُ التَّقْوى مِنْكُمْ "but it is your godwariness (التقوى) that reaches Him" (22:37).

It is also said that what is meant are the stations of proximity (مقامات القرب) to which the believers ascend through faith (الإيمان) and righteous deeds. The Exalted said: هُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَاللهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ "They have ranks with Allah, and Allah watches what they do" (3:163), and He said: لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ "They have ranks with their Lord, and forgiveness and a noble provision" (8:4), and He said: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ "Exalter of ranks, Lord of the Throne" (40:15).

The truth is that these two views return to the first view, and the mentioned ranks are real (حقيقية), not imaginary or conventional (وهمية اعتبارية).

His saying, the Exalted: تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose span is fifty thousand years" (70:4). What is meant by this day is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), as indicated by the context of the subsequent verses.

The meaning of this day's span being fifty thousand years, as they have mentioned, is that if it were to occur in the world and be applied to the time current therein, its span of time would be fifty thousand years of the years of this world.

What is meant by the ascent (عروج) of the angels and the Spirit to Him on that day is their return to Him, the Exalted, when everything returns to Him. For the Day of Resurrection is the day of the manifestation of the falling away of intermediaries (الوسائط), the severing of causes (الأسباب), and the lifting of the links between them and their effects. The angels are intermediaries entrusted with the affairs of the world and the events of the cosmos. When the causes are severed from their effects, and Allah separates them, and everything returns to Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), they return to Him and ascend their paths of ascent, surrounding the Throne of their Lord and standing in ranks. The Exalted said: وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne" (39:75), and He said: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in a row" (78:38).

It is apparent that what is meant by the Spirit (الروح) is the Spirit that is from His command (أمره), as He said: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي "Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85), and it is other than the angels, as is apparent from His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command" (16:2).

Therefore, no regard is given to what is said that the Spirit refers to Gabriel (جبرئيل), even if he is called "the Trustworthy Spirit" (الروح الأمين) and "the Holy Spirit" (روح القدس) in His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ "The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down to your heart" (26:193) and His saying: قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord" (16:102); for the restricted (المقيد) is not the same as the absolute (المطلق).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ صَبْراً جَمِيلاً "So be patient with a graceful patience" (70:5). Since the petitioner's asking for punishment was out of obstinacy (تعنت) and arrogance (استكبار), which is difficult to bear, He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to be patient and described it as "graceful" (جميل). Graceful patience (الجميل من الصبر) is that which contains no trace of anxiety (الجزع) or complaint (الشكوى). He justified this by the fact that the day, with the punishment it contains, is near.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيداً وَنَراهُ قَرِيباً "Indeed they see it to be far, and We see it to be near" (70:6-7). The pronouns in "they see it" (يَرَوْنَهُ) and "We see it" (نَراهُ) refer to the punishment or to the Day of Resurrection with the punishment bound to happen therein. The former is supported by His subsequent saying: يَوْمَ تَكُونُ السَّماءُ كَالْمُهْلِ "The day when the sky will be like molten copper" (70:8) etc.

What is meant by "seeing" (الرؤية) is belief (الاعتقاد) by way of a metaphorical consideration. Their seeing it as "far" (بعيدا) is their thinking that it is far from possibility; for asking for punishment from Allah, the Glorified, out of arrogance toward His religion and rejection of His judgment, is incompatible with belief in the Return (المعاد), even if the petitioner utters it. His, the Exalted, seeing it as "near" (قريبا) is His knowledge of its realization, and everything that is coming is near.

In these two verses is the justification for His command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to have graceful patience; for bearing harm and being patient over hardships becomes easy for a person when he is certain that relief is near and remembers that. The speech is in the sense of our saying: "So be patient over their obstinacy and arrogance in their asking for punishment with a graceful patience that is not tainted by anxiety or complaint, for We know that the punishment is near, contrary to what they deem far, and Our knowledge does not fail to match reality; rather, it is reality itself."

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَكُونُ السَّماءُ كَالْمُهْلِ "The day when the sky will be like molten copper" (70:8). Al-Muhl (المهل) is molten metal (المذاب من المعدنيات) such as copper, gold, and others. It is also said to be the dregs of oil (دردي الزيت), and it is said to be the sediment of tar (عكر القطران). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) is connected to His saying: "bound to happen" (واقِعٍ), as provided by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ "and the mountains like [tufts of] dyed wool" (70:9). Al-'Ihn (العهن) is wool in general (مطلق الصوف), and perhaps what is meant is teased wool (المنفوش), as in His saying: وَتَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ "and the mountains will be like carded wool" (101:5). It is also said to be red wool, and it is said to be wool dyed in various colors because the mountains have different colors; among them are white and red streaks and others jet black.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَسْئَلُ حَمِيمٌ حَمِيماً "and no friend will inquire about [the state of] his friend" (70:10). Al-Hamim (الحميم) is the close relative or friend whose affairs concern you and for whom you feel compassion. This is an indication of the severity of that day; for man on that day will be so occupied with himself that he will be distracted from others, such that a close friend will not ask his friend about his state due to being occupied with himself.

His saying, the Exalted: يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ "though they will be placed within each other’s sight" (70:11). The two pronouns refer to the close friends (الأحماء) known from the context. Al-Tabsir (التبصير) is to make someone see or to make something clear; that is, the close friends will be shown and made clear to one another, yet they will not ask each other about their state due to being occupied with themselves.

The sentence is a fresh start (مستأنفة) in the sense of an answer to a presumed question. It is as if when it was said, "no friend will inquire about his friend," it was asked: "Will the close friends see their friends on that day?" So it was answered: "They will be placed within each other's sight." It is also possible that "they will be placed within each other's sight" (يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ) is an attribute (صفة) for "friend" (حَمِيماً).

Among the poor interpretations is the saying of some that the meaning of "they will be placed within each other's sight" is that the angels will see the disbelievers; or what is said that the meaning is that the believers will see their enemies from among the disbelievers and the punishment they are in, and thus gloat over them; or what is said that the meaning is that the followers of misguidance will see their leaders. These are all views for which there is no evidence.

His saying, the Exalted: يَودُّ الْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِي مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمِئِذٍ بِبَنِيهِ وَصاحِبَتِهِ وَأَخِيهِ وَفَصِيلَتِهِ الَّتِي تُؤْوِيهِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ يُنْجِيهِ "The guilty one will wish he could ransom himself from the punishment of that day by his sons, and his wife and his brother, and his clan that sheltered him, and everyone who is on the earth, then [wish] that it might deliver him" (70:11-14). It is said in al-Majma': "Affection (المودة) is shared between wishing (التمني) and loving (المحبة). It is said: 'I loved the thing,' meaning I wished for it, and 'I loved it,' meaning I had affection for it; the same verb is used for both." End quote. It is possible that its use in the sense of wishing is by way of implication (تضمين).

He also said: "Ransoming (الافتداء) is to avert harm from something by means of a substitute for it." End quote. He said: "The clan (الفصيلة) is the group that is separated from the whole of the tribe by returning to a specific fatherhood rather than a general fatherhood." End quote. Some mentioned that the fasilah is his nearest kinsfolk from whom he was separated, like the immediate ancestors.

The context of these verses is one of transition (إضراب) and progression (الترقي) relative to His saying: "and no friend will inquire about his friend." It provides that the guilty one (المجرم) reaches such a degree of severity of punishment that he wishes he could ransom himself from the punishment with his most beloved and dearest relatives: his sons, his wife, his brother, his clan, and everyone on earth, and then that this ransom might deliver him. He wishes for this, let alone not asking about the state of his close friend.

The meaning is: "the guilty one" (الْمُجْرِمُ)—who is the one clothed in crime, more general than the disbeliever—"will wish" (يَوَدُّ) and desire "if he could ransom himself from the punishment of that day" (لَوْ يَفْتَدِي مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمِئِذٍ), and this is what he wishes for; the clause stands in the place of the object of "wishes." "[By] his sons" (بِبَنِيهِ) who are the most beloved of people to him; "and his wife" (وَصاحِبَتِهِ) who was a source of tranquility for him and whom he loved, and perhaps preferred over his parents; "and his brother" (وَأَخِيهِ) who was his sibling and helper; "and his clan" (وَفَصِيلَتِهِ) from his nearest kinsfolk "that sheltered him" (الَّتِي تُؤْوِيهِ) and gathered him to them; "and everyone who is on the earth" (وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً) from among the rational beings; "then [wish] that it might deliver him" (ثُمَّ يُنْجِيهِ), i.e., this ransoming.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّها لَظى نَزَّاعَةً لِلشَّوى تَدْعُوا مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّى وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعى "No indeed! It is a flaming fire, which strips away the limbs. It will summon him who turned his back and went away, and amassed [wealth] and hoarded [it]" (70:15-18). Kalla (كَلَّا) is for rebuff (الردع). The pronoun in "Indeed it" (إِنَّها) refers to Hell (جهنم) or the Fire (النار). It is called Laza (لَظى) because it blazes and flames (تتلظى وتشتعل). Nazza'ah (النزاعة) is an intensive noun from naz' (النزع), meaning uprooting or tearing away. Al-Shawa (الشوى) refers to the extremities (الأطراف) such as the hands and legs. It is said: "He shot him and hit his shawa," meaning he hit his extremities; so said al-Raghib. Hoarding wealth (إيعاء المال) is to keep it in a vessel (وعاء).

So His saying: "No indeed!" (كَلَّا) is a rebuff of his wish for deliverance from the punishment through ransoming. This rebuff is justified by His saying: "It is a flaming fire" (إِنَّها لَظى) etc. The gist of it is that Hell is a blazing fire that burns the extremities; its nature is that it seeks out the guilty to punish them, so it is not diverted from them by any ransom whatsoever.

His saying: "Indeed it is a flaming fire" (إِنَّها لَظى) means a fire whose attribute is flaming; it does not deviate from its nature nor is it extinguished. His saying: "which strips away the limbs" (نَزَّاعَةً لِلشَّوى) means its attribute is burning the extremities and tearing them away; its effect upon the one it punishes is never nullified.

His saying: "It will summon him who turned his back and went away, and amassed and hoarded" (تَدْعُوا مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّى وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعى) means it seeks out whoever turned away from the divine call to faith in Allah, turned away from His worship, and amassed wealth and kept it in his vessel, not spending from it for the petitioner and the deprived.

This meaning is what suits the context of the subsequent exception and the mention of prayer (الصلاة) and spending (الإنفاق) therein.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Al-Sayyid Abu al-Hamd narrated to us, saying: Al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated to us, and he traced the chain of transmission to Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq, from his fathers عليهم‌السلام, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appointed Ali and said: 'Whomever I am his master (مولاه), then Ali is his master,' that news flew through the lands. Then al-Nu'man ibn al-Harith al-Fihri came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

He said: "You commanded us from Allah to bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Messenger of Allah, and you commanded us to Jihad, Pilgrimage, Fasting, Prayer, and Alms-tax, and we accepted them. Then you were not satisfied until you appointed this youth and said: 'Whomever I am his master, then Ali is his master.' Is this something from you or a command from Allah?" He replied: "By Allah, besides Whom there is no god, this is from Allah."

So al-Nu'man ibn al-Harith turned away saying: "O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down on us stones from the sky." Then Allah struck him with a stone on his head and killed him, and Allah, the Exalted, revealed: سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِعٍ "A petitioner asked for a punishment bound to happen."

I say: This meaning is narrated through more than one path from the paths of the Shi'a. Some have rejected the hadith on the grounds that it is fabricated because Surah al-Ma'arij is Meccan; however, you have already seen the discussion regarding the Meccan nature of the Surah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Al-Faryabi, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: سَأَلَ سائِلٌ "A petitioner asked," he said: "It was al-Nadr ibn al-Harith. He said: 'O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down on us stones from the sky.'"

In the same source: Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi regarding His saying: سَأَلَ سائِلٌ, he said: "It was revealed in Mecca regarding al-Nadr ibn al-Harith, who had said: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ 'O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You...' the verse. And his punishment was on the day of Badr."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from others besides al-Suddi. In some of their narrations, the one who said: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ the verse, was al-Harith ibn 'Alqamah, a man from Abd al-Dar. In some of them, the petitioner for punishment was Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, who asked for it on the day of Badr, which necessitates the Medinan nature of the Surah. What is relied upon in any case is the revelation of the Surah after the saying of the one who said: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ the verse. The discussion regarding the context of the verse has already preceded.

In Amali of al-Shaykh, with his chain to Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام in a hadith: "Indeed, take account of yourselves before you are taken to account; for in the Resurrection there are fifty stations (موقفا), each station being like a thousand years of what you count." Then he recited this verse: فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ "in a day whose span is fifty thousand years."

I say: This meaning is narrated in Rawdat al-Kafi from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from him عليه‌السلام.

In al-Majma': Abu Sa'id al-Khudri narrated, saying: It was said to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "How long this day is!" He replied: "By Him in Whose hand is the soul of Muhammad, it will be lightened for the believer until it is lighter for him than an obligatory prayer (صلاة مكتوبة) he performs in the world."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several collections from Abu Sa'id from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَكُونُ السَّماءُ كَالْمُهْلِ, he said: "Molten lead and copper; likewise the sky will melt."

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ, he says: "They will recognize them, then they will not ask one another."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: نَزَّاعَةً لِلشَّوى, he said: "It tears out his eye and blackens his face."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: تَدْعُوا مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّى, he said: "It drags him toward it."

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا (١٩)
Indeed man has been created covetous: (19)
إِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ جَزُوعًا (٢٠)
anxious when an ill befalls him (20)
وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلْخَيْرُ مَنُوعًا (٢١)
and grudging [charity] when good comes his way (21)
إِلَّا ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٢٢)
—[all are such] except the prayerful, (22)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ دَآئِمُونَ (٢٣)
those who persevere in their prayers (23)
وَٱلَّذِينَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَّعْلُومٌ (٢٤)
and there is a known share in whose wealth (24)
لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (٢٥)
for the beggar and the deprived, (25)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٢٦)
and who affirm the Day of Retribution, (26)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ (٢٧)
and those who are apprehensive of the punishment of their Lord (27)
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ (٢٨)
(there is indeed no security from the punishment of their Lord) (28)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ (٢٩)
and those who guard their private parts (29)
إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ (٣٠)
(apart from their spouses and their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (30)
فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ (٣١)
but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors) (31)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ (٣٢)
and those who keep their trusts and covenants, (32)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِشَهَـٰدَٰتِهِمْ قَآئِمُونَ (٣٣)
and those who are observant of their testimonies, (33)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٣٤)
and those who are watchful of their prayers. (34)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ مُّكْرَمُونَ (٣٥)
They will be in gardens, held in honour. (35)

(Commentary)

The verses point toward the primary cause that incites man toward the vices of turning away, fleeing, hoarding, and withholding—actions that lead him to enter the eternal Fire, which is a raging flame (لظى) that strips away the scalp (نزاعة للشوى), as mentioned in the verses.

That cause is the trait of anxious-greed (الهلع) — the intense innate drive for acquisition and self-preservation — which Divine Wisdom necessitated that man be created upon, so that he might be guided by it toward what contains his goodness and happiness. However, man corrupts this trait within himself and misuses it in the pursuit of his happiness, such that it leads him to perpetual destruction—except for those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات), for they are in gardens, held in high honor.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً "Indeed man was created anxious-greedy: (19) impatient when ill befalls him, (20) and grudging when good befalls him" (70:19-21). The term "anxious-greedy" (الهلوع) is an adjective derived from al-hala‘ (الهلع), which denotes intense greed or covetousness. It has also been mentioned that the term is explained by the two verses following it; thus, he is "impatient" (الجزوع) when evil touches him and "grudging/withholding" (المنوع) when good touches him. This is a sound explanation and fits the context.

This is because the intense greed (الحرص الشديد) upon which man is naturally disposed (جبل عليه) is not a greed for everything—whether good or evil, beneficial or harmful—but rather a greed for what is good and beneficial. Furthermore, it is not a greed for every good or benefit regardless of whether it relates to him or not, or whether it belongs to him or another; rather, it is his greed for what he perceives as good for himself or beneficial in the path of goodness. A necessary consequence of this greed is that instability and agitation appear in him when "evil" (الشر)—which is the opposite of good—touches him, and that he refuses to let go of "good" (الخير) when he possesses it, prioritizing himself over others unless he perceives that letting go is better and more beneficial for his state. Thus, impatience (الجزع) when evil touches and withholding (المنع) when good touches are among the necessary concomitants of anxious-greed (الهلع) and intense greed.

Anxious-greed (الهلع) and intense greed (الحرص الشديد) upon which man is naturally disposed—which is a branch of self-love (حب الذات)—is not, in itself, a blameworthy vice. How could it be, when it is the sole means that impels man toward attaining his happiness and the perfection of his existence? It only becomes a blameworthy vice if man mismanages it.

Thus, he employs it where he should and where he should not, rightfully and wrongfully, just like all other psychological traits (الصفات النفسانية) which are noble as long as they adhere to the limit of moderation (الاعتدال), but when they deviate toward the side of excess (الإفراط) or deficiency (التفريط), they turn into a despicable vice.

In the beginning of his development, while he is a child, man perceives what he considers good for himself or evil for himself through the emotional instincts (الغرائز العاطفة) with which he has been equipped. These are what his soul desires and his faculties crave, without him limiting them by any boundary or measuring them by any standard. Thus, he becomes impatient (يجزع) if pain or any disliked thing touches him, and he withholds (يمنع) from whoever competes with him for what he holds with all his power, such as crying and the like.

He remains in this state until he is granted intellect (العقل) and maturity (الرشد), at which point he perceives truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), and good (الخير) and evil (الشر). His soul acknowledges what he has perceived, and at that moment, many of the instances (مصاديق) of truth, falsehood, good, and evil change for him. Much of what he used to see as good for himself becomes evil in his view, and vice versa.

If he persists in what he was upon—following the whims of the soul (أهواء النفس) and being devoted to its cravings, becoming occupied with them away from following the truth and being heedless of it—his heart becomes sealed. He then encounters no truth except that he refutes it, and no possessor of a right except that he oppresses him. But if Divine Care (العناية الإلهية) reaches him, that greed he had for what the soul desires turns into a greed for the truth. Thus, he does not act arrogantly toward a truth he encounters, nor does he withhold from a possessor of a right his due.

So man, in his initial state—the period of childhood before reaching maturity (البلوغ) and discernment (الرشد)—is equipped with intense greed for good, which is a trait of perfection (صفة كمالية) for him according to his state, by which he is motivated to seek good and avoid evil. The Exalted said: وَإِنَّهُ لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ "and indeed he is passionate in his love of good" (100:8).

Then, when he is granted maturity and discernment, he is increased with another equipment, which is the intellect (العقل) — the faculty of discernment that perceives the realities of things as they truly are. Through it, he perceives what constitutes true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and what constitutes goodness in action. His intense greed for "good" and his being impatient when evil touches and withholding when good touches are transformed from greed for worldly good into intense greed for the actual good (الخير الواقعي). This manifests as anguish and fear when an otherworldly evil (شر أخروي) touches him—which is sin (المعصية)—and racing toward the forgiveness of his Lord when an otherworldly good (خير أخروي) touches him—which is encountering a virtuous deed (الحسنة). As for worldly evil and good, he does not exceed in them what Allah has bounded for him of patience (الصبر) during calamity, patience in obedience, and patience against sin. This trait is a trait of perfection for this human being.

However, if man turns away from what his intellect perceives and his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — acknowledges, and devotes himself to following whims (الهوى), embracing falsehood, and encroaching upon the right of every possessor of a right, and does not stop his greed for good at any limit—

Then he has exchanged the blessing of Allah for a curse. He has taken an instinctive trait (صفة غريزية) that Allah created as a means for him to reach the happiness of this world and the Hereafter, and turned it into a means toward wretchedness (الشقوة) and destruction (الهلكة), driving him toward turning away, fleeing, hoarding, and withholding, as mentioned in the verses.

It has become clear from what preceded that there is no harm in attributing man's anxious-greed (هلع) in the verses to creation (الخلقة), even though the speech is intended for blame. The Exalted has said: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ "who perfected everything He created" (32:7). This is because the blame that attaches to it is only from the side of man and his mismanagement, not from His side, the Exalted. It is like all His other blessings upon man, which he turns into curses through his poor choice (سوء اختياره).

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned, in an attempt to escape this difficulty, that there is a metaphor (استعارة) in the speech. The meaning is that man, due to his preference for impatience and withholding and their firm rootedness in him, is as if he were naturally disposed and imprinted upon them, and as if it were a created matter within him that is necessary and non-voluntary. Thus, the speech is based on a comparison (التشبيه), not to indicate that it is truly created by Allah, because the speech is intended for blame, and Allah, the Glorified, does not blame His own act. Among the proofs for this is the exception of the believers who struggled against their souls and were saved from both impatience and withholding.

The response to this is that the trait is created as a blessing and a virtue, and it is man who removes it from the realm of virtue to vice, and from blessing to curse. The blame returns to the trait from the aspect of his mismanagement, not from the aspect of it being His act, the Exalted.

The exception of the believers is not because the trait is not created within them, but because they kept it upon its perfection and did not exchange it for vice and curse.

It has also been answered regarding the exception that it is a "disconnected exception" (منقطع), but this is as you can see [weak].

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ "except the prayerful" (70:22). This is an exception from "man" described with anxious-greed. In the precedence given to prayer (الصلاة) over the other righteous deeds enumerated in the following verses, there is an indication of its nobility and that it is the best of deeds.

Furthermore, it has a prominent effect in repelling the blameworthy vice of anxious-greed. The Exalted has said: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ "Indeed the prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs" (29:45).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ "those who are persevering in their prayers" (70:23). In the annexation (إضافة) of prayer to the pronoun, there is an indication that they are constant in the prayers they perform, whatever they may be, not that they are always in a state of prayer. In this, there is an indication that an action only completes its effect through constancy (المداومة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ "and those in whose wealth there is a known right (24) for the beggar and the deprived" (70:24-25). Some have interpreted it as the obligatory alms (الزكاة المفروضة). However, in the hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The known right (الحق المعلوم) is not from the zakat—rather, it is a known amount they spend for the poor." The "beggar" (السائل) is the poor person who asks, and the "deprived" (المحروم) is the poor person who remains chaste and does not ask. The context is not devoid of supporting this, for the zakat has specific categories named in His saying: إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ "The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller. [This is] an ordinance from Allah" (9:60), and it is not restricted to the beggar and the deprived as is apparent in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ "and those who confirm the Day of Retribution" (70:26). What the context of enumerating righteous deeds suggests is that the meaning of their "confirming" (تصديقهم) the Day of Retribution is practical confirmation (التصديق العملي) rather than just creedal confirmation (التصديق الاعتقادي). This is such that their conduct in life is the conduct of one who sees that whatever action he performs, he will be held accountable for it and recompensed for it—if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

In the expression يُصَدِّقُونَ (they confirm), there is an indication of continuity (الاستمرار); thus, it is the constant self-vigilance (المراقبة الدائمة) by remembering Him, the Exalted, at every action they face, such that they perform what He desires and abandon what He dislikes.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ "and those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord" (70:27). That is, they are afraid. The discussion regarding their fear (إشفاقهم) of the punishment of their Lord is similar to the discussion regarding their confirmation of the Day of Retribution; it is the practical fear (الإشفاق العملي) apparent from their state.

A necessary consequence of their fear of the punishment of their Lord—alongside their adherence to righteous deeds and their striving for the sake of Allah—is that they do not place absolute reliance in the righteous deeds they perform, nor do they feel secure (يأمنوا) from the punishment of Allah, for security (الأمن) does not coexist with fear.

The criterion for fear of the punishment is that punishment is for opposition; thus, there is no salvation from it except through the soul's obedience. Yet there is no reliance upon the soul, as it has no power in its essence except what Allah has empowered it with, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner (المالك) who is not owned. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً "Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah’" (5:17).

Furthermore, although Allah, the Glorified, has promised the people of obedience salvation and stated that He does not break His promise, the promise does not restrict the absolute nature of His power (إطلاق قدرته). He remains, despite that, Powerful over what He wills, and His will is effective. Thus, there is no "security" in the sense of the negation of the power to do what contradicts the promise; therefore, fear remains in its place. For this reason, we see that He, the Exalted, says regarding His angels: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ "They fear their Lord [who is] above them, and they do what they are commanded" (16:50), describing them with fear while He explicitly declares their infallibility (عصمتهم). He says regarding His prophets: وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ "and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah" (33:39). And He describes the believers in this verse with fear (الإشفاق) while He promises them at the end of the verses with a decisive word, saying: أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ "They will be in gardens, held in high honor" (70:35).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ "—indeed the punishment of their Lord is not something to be felt safe from—" (70:28). This is a justification for their fear of the punishment of their Lord; it clarifies that they are correct in their fear of the punishment, and the reason for this has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ — until His saying — هُمُ العادُونَ "and those who guard their private parts, (29) except from their spouses or their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (30) but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors" (70:29-31). The commentary on these three verses preceded at the beginning of Surah al-Mu’minun.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ راعُونَ "and those who keep their trusts and their covenants" (70:32). What is immediately understood from "trusts" (الأمانات) are the various types of trust with which they are entrusted, such as wealth and other things bequeathed regarding a person or honor. Their "observance" (رعايتهم) of them is that they protect them and do not betray them. It is said: because of the many types of trusts, the plural form was used, unlike "covenant" (العهد).

It is also said: what is meant by them is everything Allah has charged them with of belief and action; thus, it encompasses the rights of Allah and the rights of people. If they were to neglect any of them, they would have betrayed it.

It is also said: every blessing Allah has given His servant, such as limbs and other things, is a trust (أمانة). Whoever uses any of them for other than what Allah gave it for and permitted its use in, has betrayed it.

The apparent meaning of "covenant" (العهد) is a person's contract with another, by word or deed, regarding a matter. Its "observance" is to keep it and not break it without a justifying cause.

It is also said: the covenant is everything a person commits to for another. Thus, the servant's faith in his Lord is a covenant from him by which he covenanted his Lord to obey Him in all He charged him with. If he were to disobey Him in anything He commanded or forbade, he would have broken his covenant.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِشَهاداتِهِمْ قائِمُونَ "and those who are upright in their testimonies" (70:33). Testimony (الشهادة) is well-known. "Standing by the testimony" (القيام بالشهادة) is the lack of refusal to bear it and the performance of what was borne exactly as it was borne, without concealment or alteration. The verses on this meaning are numerous.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ "and those who are watchful of their prayers" (70:34). What is meant by "watchfulness" (المحافظة) over the prayer is observing its qualities of perfection according to what the Law (الشرع) has exhorted.

It is said: watchfulness over the prayer is different from constancy (الدوام) in it. Constancy relates to the prayer itself, while watchfulness relates to its quality. Thus, there is no repetition in mentioning watchfulness over it after mentioning constancy in it.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ "They will be in gardens, held in high honor" (70:35). The indication is to the "prayerful" in His saying: إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ (except the prayerful). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "gardens" (جنات) is for magnification (التفخيم). "In gardens" (فِي جَنَّاتٍ) is the predicate (خبر), and "held in high honor" (مُكْرَمُونَ) is a second predicate, or the phrase "in gardens" is an adverbial (ظرف) for His saying "held in high honor."

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً (impatient when ill befalls him), he said: "Evil (الشر) is poverty and destitution." Regarding وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً (and grudging when good befalls him), he said: "Wealth and abundance."

In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: "Then He made an exception and said: إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ (except the prayerful), describing them by the best of their deeds: الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ (those who are persevering in their prayers). He says: If he makes something of the supererogatory prayers (النوافل) obligatory upon himself, he persists in it."

I say: His saying "If he makes... obligatory upon himself" etc.—the Imam (peace be upon him) derived this meaning from the annexation of prayer to the pronoun "their" (هُمْ), and we have pointed to this previously.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ (and those who are watchful of their prayers). He said: "It is the obligatory prayer (الفريضة)." I said: الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ (those who are persevering in their prayers). He said: "It is the supererogatory prayer (النافلة)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ (and those in whose wealth there is a known right). It is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "The known right is not from the zakat—it is something you take out from your wealth, if you wish every Friday, and if you wish every day; and for every possessor of merit is his merit."

He said: It is also narrated from him that he said: "It is that you maintain ties with kinship, give to the one who deprived you, and give charity to the one who showed enmity toward you."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Kafi from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) through several paths, and it was narrated in al-Mahasin from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Safwan al-Jammal from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ (for the beggar and the deprived). He said: "The deprived (المحروم) is the one whose livelihood is constrained (المحارف)—the one who has been deprived of the earning of his right hand in buying and selling."

He said: In another narration from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), they said: "The deprived is the man whose intellect has no defect, but his provision has not been expanded for him, and he is one whose livelihood is constrained (محارف)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ (and those who are watchful of their prayers). Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl narrated from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) that he said: "Those are the performers of the fifty prayers from among our followers (شيعتنا)."

I say: Perhaps this is based on what has been narrated from them (peace be upon them) that the legislation of the daily supererogatory prayers is to complete the obligatory ones.

Ayahs

فَمَالِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ (٣٦)
What is the matter with the faithless that they scramble toward you, (36)
عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ عِزِينَ (٣٧)
from left and right, in groups? (37)
أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ (٣٨)
Does each man among them hope to enter the garden of bliss? (38)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
Never! Indeed, We created them from what they know. (39)
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ وَٱلْمَغَـٰرِبِ إِنَّا لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٤٠)
So I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests that We are able (40)
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٤١)
to replace them with [others] better than them and We are not to be outmaneuvered. (41)
فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٤٢)
So leave them to gossip and play till they encounter the day they are promised: (42)
يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ سِرَاعًا كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ (٤٣)
the day when they emerge from graves, hastening, as if racing toward a target, (43)
خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (٤٤)
with a humbled look [in their eyes], overcast by abasement. That is the day they had been promised. (44)

(Commentary)

In the first section of the verses of this Surah, following the mention of their request for the punishment, the Glorified mentioned that they shall face a befalling punishment that none can avert. This is the blazing Fire, which drags away the scalp, calling those who turned their backs and retreated, and who amassed wealth and hoarded it.

Then, in the second section, He explained the underlying cause of their affliction with this wretchedness: that man is equipped with the instinct of impatience (الهلع) — the innate tendency toward anxiety and greed — and the love of his own well-being. Following one's whims (اتباع الهوى) in the exercise of this instinct leads to arrogance against every truth one encounters, thereby casting him into the eternal Fire. None shall escape this except those who are righteous in deed, who confirm the Day of Judgment, and who are apprehensive of the punishment of their Lord.

In this section of verses—which is the third section—He turns back toward those disbelievers (الكفار) as if in wonder at their state, as they gather around the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hastening toward him from the right and from the left in separate groups, fixing their gaze upon him and never leaving him. He addressed him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: What is the matter with them that they surround you, hastening toward you and clinging to you? Does every man among them desire to enter a Garden of Bliss while he is a disbeliever, when Allah, the Glorified, has already decreed that He shall not honor with His Garden anyone except those believers whom He has excepted? Do they intend to outstrip Allah and frustrate Him by nullifying what He has judged and voiding what He has decreed? By no means! Indeed, Allah, who created them from a lowly sperm-drop, is capable of replacing them with those better than them, and of creating from that which He created them, others who will worship Him and enter His Garden.

Then He commanded the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to cut off their disputation and leave them to indulge and play until they meet their Day which they are promised.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما لِالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ عِزِينَ "What is the matter with the faithless, that they gallop toward you, (36) from the right and the left, in groups?" (70:36-37). Al-Tabarsi said in al-Majma': "Al-Zajjaj said: The 'muhti'' (المهطع) is the one who fixes his gaze upon something without averting it, and this is characteristic of the enemy's gaze. Abu Ubaydah said: 'al-Ihta'' (الإهطاع) means to hasten. 'Izin' (عزين) refers to groups in separation; the singular is 'izzah' (عزة)." End quote. The word 'qibal' (قبل) with a kasrah then a fathah means the direction adjacent to something. The 'fa' (ف) in 'fama' (فما) is elided (فصيحة) — indicating a conditional meaning.

The meaning is: If the destiny of man, due to his disbelief and arrogance against the truth, is the Fire—except for those believers who were excepted—then what is the matter with those who disbelieve that they are in your presence, fixing their gaze upon you and not lifting their eyes from you, while they are scattered groups to your right and your left? Do they hope to enter the Garden, thereby frustrating Allah and outstripping Him in what He has decreed—that none shall enter the Garden except the righteous among the believers?

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ "Does every one of them hope to be admitted into a garden of bliss?" (70:38). The interrogation is for denunciation (الإنكار). That is: What is it that drives them to surround you and hasten toward you? Does the hope of each of them to enter a Garden of Bliss drive them to this, while he is a disbeliever? For a disbeliever has no hope of entering the Garden.

The hope (الطمع) is attributed to "every man" (كل امرئ) among them rather than to their collective group—by saying "do they hope to enter..."—just as the hastening (الإهطاع) was attributed to their collective group by saying "muhti'in" (مهطعين). This is because the hope for happiness and success that is beneficial is the hope residing within the individual's soul, which motivates him toward faith and righteous deeds, rather than that which resides in the group qua group. The hope of the collective, as a collective, does not suffice for the happiness of each individual.

In His saying: أَنْ يُدْخَلَ "to be admitted," using the passive voice (مجهولا) from the form of if'al (الإفعال), there is an indication that their entry into the Garden is not contingent upon their own choice or will. Rather, if it were to happen, it would be up to Allah, the Glorified; He is the One who admits them into the Garden if He wills, and He will never admit them given His decree that no disbeliever shall enter it.

It has been said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to pray near the Kaaba and recite the Quran, and the polytheists would gather around him in circles and groups, listening and mocking his words, saying: "If these people enter the Garden as Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله says, then we shall surely enter it before them." Thus, the verses were revealed.

However, this view does not align with the context (سياق) of the verses, which clearly shows that their action is a consequence of what preceded regarding the deprivation of people from entering the Garden except for the excepted believers. For it is necessary, based on this view, that their gathering around him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and their hastening toward him was driven only by their excessive enmity and their exaggeration in harming and insulting him. Their saying, "We shall enter the Garden before the believers"—while they were polytheists persisting in the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد) and not acknowledging either Fire or Garden—would have been merely mockery and sarcasm (تهكما).

Thus, there would be no grounds for making their action a consequence of the preceding discourse on the Fire, the Garden, and the question—in a context of wonder—regarding the reason driving them to it, followed by the interrogation and denunciation of their hope to enter the Garden.

Therefore, what was mentioned previously supports the view that the "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا) in His saying: فَما لِالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا refers to a group of hypocrites (المنافقين) who believed in him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله outwardly and accompanied him, but then disbelieved by rejecting some of what was revealed to him. This is indicated by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا فَطُبِعَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ "That is because they believed and then disbelieved, so their hearts were sealed" (63:3), and His saying: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ "Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your faith" (9:66), and His saying: فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ "So He caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts" (9:77).

These were a people who had believed and entered the community of believers, accompanying the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hastening toward him from the right and the left in groups. Then they disbelieved in some of what was revealed to him, showing no concern for it. So Allah, the Glorified, rebuked them in these verses, stating that they do not benefit from accompanying him, nor do they have any right to hope for entry into the Garden; they are not among those who shall enter it, nor can they outstrip or frustrate Him.

This is supported by the subsequent verse: إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ "Indeed We are able to replace them with those better than them," as we shall point out.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ "No indeed! We created them from what they know." (70:39). This is a deterrent (ردع) against their hope of entering the Garden while in a state of disbelief.

In His saying: إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ "We created them from what they know," what they know refers to the sperm-drop (النطفة), for man is created from it.

The speech is linked to what follows, and the whole serves as a justification (تعليل) for the deterrent. The gist of the justification is: We created them from a sperm-drop—and they know this—so We have the power to take them away and create in their place another people who are better than them, who are believers and do not reject anything of the religion of Allah. We are not outstripped such that these disbelievers could frustrate Us and outstrip Us, forcing Us to admit them into the Garden, which would nullify Our decree that no disbeliever shall enter the Garden.

It has been said: The "min" (من) in His saying: مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ "from what they know" conveys the meaning of the "lam" of justification (لام التعليل). The meaning would be: We created them for the sake of what they know—which is perfection through faith and obedience. Thus, it is mandatory that they clothe themselves with these so that We may admit them into the Garden. How then can they hope to enter it while they are disbelievers? They only came to know this through the report of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

It has also been said: The "min" is for the beginning of an end (ابتداء الغاية). The meaning would be: We created them from a filthy sperm-drop that does not suit the realm of sanctity and purity until it is purified through faith and obedience and takes on the character of the angels to enter [the Garden]. How can they achieve that while they are disbelievers?

It has also been said: The "ma" (ما) in مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ refers to the genus (الجنس). The meaning would be: We created them from the genus of human beings who know, or from the creation that knows, not from the genus of animals that do not reason or understand. Thus, the proof is established against them and is complete. These three views are weak (سخيفة).

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ "I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests that We are able (40) to replace them with [others] better than them, and We are not to be outmaneuvered." (70:40-41). What is meant by the easts and the wests (المشارق والمغارب) are the points of sunrise and sunset, for the sun has a different point of rising and setting for every day of the solar year, to which it does not return until the same day of the following year. It is also possible that what is meant are the rising and setting points of all the stars.

In this verse, despite its brevity, there are several instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات). In His saying: فَلا أُقْسِمُ "I swear," there is a shift from the first-person plural (التكلم مع الغير) in "We created them" (إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ) to the first-person singular. The reason for this is to emphasize the oath by attributing it to Allah Himself.

In His saying: بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ "by the Lord of the easts and the wests," there is a shift from the first-person singular to the third-person (الغيبة). The reason for this is to point to an attribute among His attributes that is the principle (المبدأ) in the creation of people generation after generation, which is His Lordship (ربوبيته) over the easts and the wests. For the rising after rising and setting after setting, which is inseparable from the passage of time, has a complete role in the formation of man generation after generation and all other terrestrial events associated with it.

In His saying: إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ "Indeed We are able," there is a shift from the third-person to the first-person plural. The reason for this is to indicate the grandeur (العظمة) appropriate for the mention of power (القدرة). In the mention of His Lordship over the easts and the wests, there is an indication of the justification for this power; for He to whom the governance (تدبير) of events in their formation traces back cannot be frustrated by any of the events—which are His own acts—from achieving any of them. Nor can anything of His creation prevent Him from replacing it with something better than it; otherwise, the preventer would share in the matter of governance, and Allah, the Glorified, is One, having no partner in His Lordship. Understand this well.

In His saying: إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ "Indeed We are able to replace them with those better than them," the "ala" (عَلى) is connected to "qadirun" (لَقادِرُونَ). The first object of "nubaddila" (نبدل) is an omitted pronoun referring back to them; it was omitted to indicate the insignificance of their affair and the lack of concern for them. "Khayran" (خَيْراً) is its second object, an adjective standing in place of its noun. The estimation is: "We are able to replace them with a people better than them." Their being better than them lies in their believing in Allah and not disbelieving in Him, and in their following the truth and not rejecting it.

In His saying: وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ "and We are not to be outmaneuvered," the meaning of "sabq" (السبق) — outstripping — is being overcome, used metaphorically. For Allah to be "outmaneuvered" would mean that His creation of them prevents Him from taking them away and bringing in their stead a people better than them.

The context of the verse is not devoid of some support for what was previously mentioned—that the "those who disbelieve" refers to a group of hypocrites rather than the polytheists who were antagonistic to the religion and denied the very basis of the Resurrection. For the outward meaning of His saying: خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ "better than them" is not devoid of an indication or a hint that there is some trace of goodness in them, and Allah may replace them with those better than them. As for the polytheists, there is no good in them; however, this group of hypocrites—their preservation of the outward forms of the religion they believed in and did not reject—is not devoid of some benefit for Islam.

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying: إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ until the end of the three verses is a justification for the deterrent in His saying: كَلَّا "No indeed!" The summary of the content of the three verses is that they are created from a sperm-drop—and they know this—and it is an ongoing creation. Allah, who is the Lord of ongoing events, among which is the creation of man generation after generation, and the Governor of them, is able to take them away and replace them with those better than them who would care for the matter of religion and be worthy of entering the Garden. Their creation does not prevent Him from replacing them with those better than them and admitting them into the Garden due to the perfection of their faith, without being compelled to admit these [hypocrites] into the Garden. Thus, His decree that the Garden is for the righteous among the people of faith is not nullified.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ "So leave them to indulge and play until they meet their day which they are promised," (70:42). This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to leave them and what they are engaged in, not to persist in disputation with them, nor to exhaust himself in admonishing them. What they are engaged in has been called "indulging" (الخوض) and "playing" (اللعب) to indicate that they do not benefit from it in any real sense, despite their deep involvement and persistence in it, just like play which has no benefit beyond the imagination. So let them be left until they meet the Day they are promised, which is the Day of Resurrection.

In the attribution of the Day to them, there is an indication of a specific connection it has with them, which is the connection to their punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ "the day they emerge from the graves, hastening as if they were racing toward a target," (70:43). This is an explanation of their Day which they are promised, which is the Day of Resurrection.

"Al-ajdath" (الأجداث) is the plural of "jadath" (جدث), which is the grave. "Sira'an" (سراعا) is the plural of "sari'" (سريع) — fast. "Nusub" (نصب) is that which is set up as a sign on the road, which travelers head toward to be guided by it. It has been said: It is the idol set up for worship, but this is far-fetched in the speech of the Exalted. "Al-ifad" (الإيفاض) means to hasten. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ "with their eyes humbled, overwhelmed by abasement. That is the day they were promised." (70:44). "Khashu'" (الخشوع) — humility — is a specific state of being affected in the heart upon witnessing grandeur and majesty; its counterpart in the limbs is "khudu'" (الخضوع) — submission. The attribution of humility to the eyes is due to the appearance of its effects in them. "Al-rahaq" (الرهق) is to be forcefully overwhelmed by something.

His saying: ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ "That is the day they were promised" refers to the previously mentioned descriptions: emerging from the graves in haste, the humility of the eyes, and being overwhelmed by abasement.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that 'Abd ibn Humayd reported from 'Ubadah ibn Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله entered the mosque and said: 'Why do I see you in separate groups (عزين), in circles like the circles of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية)?' A man would sit behind his brother."

I say: It was also narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah with the wording: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came out while his companions were sitting in circles and groups, and he said: 'Why do I see you in separate groups (عزين)?'" This meaning was also narrated from Jabir ibn Samurah.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying: كَلَّا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ "No indeed! We created them from what they know," he said: "From a sperm-drop (نطفة), then a clinging mass (علقة)." Regarding His saying: فَلا أُقْسِمُ "I swear," he said: "It means 'I swear' بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ 'by the Lord of the easts and the wests'—the easts of winter and the easts of summer, and the wests of winter and the wests of summer."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Abi Hammad, reaching to the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام, who said: "It has three hundred and sixty easts and three hundred and sixty wests; the day it rises in one, it does not return to it until the following year."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying: يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً "the day they emerge from the graves, hastening," he said: "From the grave كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ 'as if they were racing toward a target'—they are called toward the caller." Regarding His saying: تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ "overwhelmed by abasement," he said: "Abasement afflicts them."
